{{Short description|Subatomic particle with negative electric charge}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Electron
| image = Cyclotron motion wider view.jpg
| caption = A beam of electrons deflected in a circle by a [[magnetic field]].
| topics = [[Electric charge]], [[Spin (physics)]], [[Quantum mechanics]], [[Pauli exclusion principle]]
| composition = [[Elementary particle]]
| statistics = [[Fermion]]
| symbol = e⁻, β⁻
| antiparticle = [[Positron]]
| mass = 9.1093837015×10⁻³¹ kg
| charge = −1 e (−1.602176634×10⁻¹⁹ C)
| spin = 1/2
}}

The '''electron''' is a [[subatomic particle]] with a negative [[electric charge]]. Electrons belong to the first generation of the [[lepton]] particle family and are generally thought to be [[elementary particle]]s because they have no known components or substructure. The electron has a mass that is approximately 1/1836 that of the [[proton]].

Electrons play an essential role in numerous physical phenomena, such as [[electricity]], [[magnetism]], [[chemical bonds]], and [[thermal conductivity]]. They also participate in the [[weak interaction]] and [[electromagnetism|electromagnetic]] interactions. An electron spontaneously decays into two [[neutrino]]s only if there is long-lived discrepancy in mass with its [[antiparticle]], the [[positron]], which has identical mass but positive charge; no such decay has been observed.

== History ==

=== Discovery ===
The electron was discovered in 1897 by [[J. J. Thomson]], who showed that [[cathode ray]]s were composed of previously unknown negatively charged particles. Thomson calculated these particles must have bodies much smaller than [[atom]]s and a very large [[charge-to-mass ratio]]. The name "electron" was coined in 1891 by [[George Johnstone Stoney]] to denote the fundamental unit of electric charge.

=== Quantum mechanics ===
The electron was the first particle for which [[wave–particle duality]] was demonstrated. In 1924, [[Louis de Broglie]] hypothesized that the electron had wave properties. This was confirmed in 1927 by the [[Davisson–Germer experiment]], which observed [[electron diffraction]]. In 1928, [[Paul Dirac]] published the [[Dirac equation]], which combined quantum mechanics with [[special relativity]] and predicted the existence of the [[positron]] and electron [[spin (physics)|spin]].

== Properties ==

=== Wave–particle duality ===
{{Main|Wave–particle duality}}
Like all quantum particles, electrons exhibit both particle and wave behavior. The [[de Broglie wavelength]] of an electron with momentum <math>p</math> is:
:<math>\lambda = \frac{h}{p}</math>
where <math>h</math> is the [[Planck constant]].

=== Spin ===
{{Main|Spin (physics)}}
The electron has an intrinsic [[angular momentum]] ([[Spin (physics)|spin]]) of <math>\hbar/2</math>, making it a [[fermion]] that obeys the [[Pauli exclusion principle]]. The electron's [[magnetic moment]] is approximately:
:<math>\boldsymbol{\mu} = -g_s \frac{e}{2m_e} \mathbf{S}</math>
where <math>g_s \approx 2.0023</math> is the electron [[spin g-factor]].

=== Quantum numbers ===
In [[atom]]s, electrons are described by four [[quantum number]]s:
# Principal quantum number <math>n</math> — determines energy
# Azimuthal quantum number <math>\ell</math> — determines orbital angular momentum
# Magnetic quantum number <math>m_\ell</math> — determines orbital orientation
# Spin quantum number <math>m_s</math> — determines spin orientation

== Role in atoms ==
{{Main|Atomic physics}}
Electrons in atoms are bound to the [[atomic nucleus]] by the [[electromagnetic force]]. They occupy discrete [[energy level]]s called [[electron shell]]s, governed by the [[Schrödinger equation]] for the atomic potential. The arrangement of electrons determines the chemical properties of [[chemical element|elements]].

=== Chemical bonding ===
Electrons in the outermost shells ([[valence electron]]s) are responsible for [[chemical bond]]s. [[Covalent bond]]s result from sharing of electrons; [[ionic bond]]s from electron transfer; and [[metallic bond]]s from a sea of delocalized electrons.

== Applications ==
* [[Electron microscope]] — using electron waves for atomic-scale imaging
* [[Cathode ray tube]] — display technology
* [[Electronics]] and [[semiconductor]]s — basis of modern computing
* [[Particle accelerator]]s — high-energy electron beams
* [[Electron beam welding]] and [[electron beam lithography|lithography]]

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Atomic nucleus]]
* [[Electric charge]]
* [[Fermion]]
* [[Lepton]]
* [[Photon]]
* [[Positron]]
* [[Proton]]
* [[Quantum mechanics]]
* [[Spin (physics)]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Electrons| ]]
[[Category:Leptons]]
[[Category:Atomic physics]]
[[Category:Quantum mechanics]]
